text: Welcome to this episode of This is My Architecture. I'm Wale from AWS and I'm joined by Kostab from L&T Technology Services.
Hi Kostab.
Hi Wale.
Could you tell me a little bit about L&T Technology Services?
So L&T Technology Services is a global leader in engineering and R&D services.
We have filed almost 416 patents with 51 of the top global ER&D spenders.
We are present in 49 different locations.
We have our engineering and R&D development centers.
We work in disruptive technologies like 5G, digital factory, artificial intelligence and automotive vehicles.
So I see that you have a lot of IoT going on.
So could you talk about how your solutions kind of bridge that IT, OT kind of gap?
Yes.
So we collect a lot of data from the plant machinery like motors and HVACs.
And we convert this data to and transform it for processing by AWS IoT Core and the Greengrass Lambda functions at the edge.
So this way we connect a lot of plant machinery and smart building equipment to the AWS IoT Core.
So what kind of equipment data do you collect and then send them to IoT Core?
In a plant, we collect data from motors.
These are very critical equipment in a data center.
We have developed models for motors, pumps and ropes.
These will predict the failure of such equipment.
We also have equipment for smart buildings, which are like HVACs and escalators.
This data is continuously collected and processed and sent to the cloud.
So once this data goes from Greengrass into IoT Core, how do you then pick how your data is then parsed and sent to your end users?
So when the data is reaching the AWS IoT Core from the edge, we have IoT rules which filter the data and send it to the IoT analytics and to the Kinesis data streams.
IoT analytics is used for batch analysis to run statistical process control functions.
IoT Core from the edge, and Kinesis streams is used in two ways for creating data set and audit log in the S3 buckets and running real-time analytics in the Kinesis analytics.
Great.
So then your end users, how do they actually get all of the information?
So how do they get things like the ability to do anomaly detections or insights into the equipment data?
So we have the real-time analytics algorithms running here, which will predict any failures.
We run condition-based monitoring.
And we also run batch analytics for statistical process control.
So this will trigger any breach of threshold, and it will be visualized in the QuickSight and also available to the end user through an alert in the SNS topic.
While he can also see the continuous monitoring in the real-time analytics dashboard, where he can see any anomalies in the operation of a machine.
So there's lots of ways that your users can be notified or visualize their data.
What are you doing to make your system kind of smarter over time?
So this is really exciting.
When Kinesis streams, collects the data at the S3, we can use this as a data lake for training our models and improving them continuously over time.
These models become more accurate and can then be updated on the edge for even better performance and detection of anomalies.
This way, the AWS Greengrass can detect any failure at the edge and then raise an alarm by sending out an SNS topic alert to the IoT core, which can then go to the end user.
Kostab, that's really awesome.
So it seems like what you have is you have everything from edge to cloud.
You're able to run compute with Lambda.
You're able to then drive analytics, either real-time or batch, through multiple services like Kinesis and IoT analytics.
Then you essentially go full circle by doing machine learning that ends up back at the edge.
That's true, Wale.
We have an end-to-end IoT platform that collects data from the edge and is visualized in the cloud.
It also provides analytics and alerts to the end user.
And it completes the entire device management lifecycle as well using the AWS IoT Core.
Well, thank you so much for diving in with us.
And thanks so much for watching.
This is my architecture.
Thank you.
Thanks.
